metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

coverage.verify_error: step 'review@then/verify_branch@then/verify' failed: the model hit the token cap before finishing (finish_reason=length) — its output is TRUNCATED; raise max_tokens or split the step

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all 10 adopted workstreams (by name/ID or inline summary) directly in this plan so an executor can verify completeness without referencing external documents.
findings.finding: The 10 adopted workstreams (child stories) — the core deliverable — are not enumerated anywhere in the plan. An executor must stop, locate the external gap report (~/dso_rebar_plan_review_gap_report.md) and reconstruct the design decisions from an out-of-band evaluation session to know what to create.
findings.location: Success Criteria line 1 and Acceptance Criteria line 1: '10 adopted workstreams (child stories)' / '10 child stories created capturing the design decisions from the evaluation session'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a numbered list under a 'Workstreams' section, e.g.: '1. R-1 part 1 — cohort field; 2. [name] — [one-line scope]; ...' mirroring the gap report's identifiers so the AC count is unambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'eval-guarded' concretely (e.g., 'at least one automated regression eval covering the changed criterion, with a pass/fail result in CI') so the criterion is in-session verifiable.
findings.finding: The Success Criterion 'eval-guarded where it changes reviewer behavior' uses an undefined threshold — what constitutes an 'eval guard', what behavior change qualifies, and what evidence suffices is not stated.
findings.location: Success Criteria line 1: 'implemented and eval-guarded where it changes reviewer behavior'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify what 'calibration evidence' means (e.g., 'a documented eval run with sample size ≥ N showing FP rate ≤ X% on the blocking criterion') or link to the existing calibration standard.
findings.finding: The Success Criterion 'No new blocking behavior ships without calibration evidence' does not define what counts as 'calibration evidence', leaving the criterion subjective and non-verifiable.
findings.location: Success Criteria line 3: 'No new blocking behavior ships without calibration evidence (advisory-by-default, consistent with existing gate posture)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace or supplement 'durable home' with a verifiable artifact criterion (e.g., 'the gap-report document links back to this epic ID' or 'all 10 child stories reference this epic as parent') so it is in-session checkable.
findings.finding: The Acceptance Criterion 'This epic is the durable home for the DSO-adoption effort' is a designation, not a measurable observable outcome — there is no deterministic in-session check that can verify it is 'done'.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria line 3: 'This epic is the durable home for the DSO-adoption effort'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile Success Criteria bullet 1 ('implemented and eval-guarded') with Acceptance Criteria bullet 1 ('created') — either tighten the AC to require implementation or soften the SC to match the epic's planning-only scope.
findings.finding: Success Criteria says each of the 10 workstreams is 'implemented and eval-guarded', but the matching Acceptance Criterion only requires the 10 child stories to be 'created' — an implementer reading both sections gets contradictory signals about what 'done' means for this epic.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 1 vs Acceptance Criteria bullet 1
findings.suggested_fix: If this epic is a planning/decomposition epic, change SC bullet 1 to 'Each of the 10 adopted workstreams has a created, design-complete child story with eval guards specified.' If it is also the delivery epic, update AC bullet 1 to 'implemented and eval-guarded.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC bullet 3 to specify that this epic is the durable home for the *adopted* (in-scope) workstreams, and that the linked epics are the homes for their respective out-of-scope items — or replace 'durable home' with 'primary tracking home with links to satellite epics.'
findings.finding: Acceptance Criteria bullet 3 states 'This epic is the durable home for the DSO-adoption effort', yet the Context section explicitly routes four significant DSO items to other epics (grumpy-gland-rose, only-crave-art, fort-wisp-wren, spin-cub-usher), meaning the adoption effort is substantially distributed elsewhere.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria bullet 3 vs Context (evaluated but NOT in this epic) section
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC bullet 3 to: 'This epic is the durable tracking home for the 10 in-scope DSO-adoption workstreams; satellite epics (grumpy-gland-rose, only-crave-art, fort-wisp-wren, spin-cub-usher) own their respective out-of-scope items.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a section (or table) to the plan body enumerating all 10 in-scope workstreams by name/ID so that AC bullet 1 ('10 child stories created') can be independently verified.
findings.finding: Acceptance Criteria bullet 1 requires '10 child stories created' but the plan body never enumerates what the 10 workstreams are — the criterion cannot be verified against the plan because the deliverables it measures are not described anywhere in the plan text.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria bullet 1; plan body (all sections)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'In-scope workstreams (10)' bullet list naming each workstream, mirroring the structure of the 'Context (evaluated but NOT in this epic)' exclusion list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Standardize on one term ('child stories' or 'workstreams') throughout the plan, or explicitly define the equivalence once in a terminology note.
findings.finding: The plan uses 'workstreams' in the Success Criteria and 'child stories' in the Acceptance Criteria to describe the same set of 10 deliverables, creating terminology inconsistency.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 1 vs Acceptance Criteria bullet 1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either describe (briefly) the mechanism and ownership for the CI drift-parity gate in the plan body, or move this criterion to the relevant child story that touches prompt/routing rubric edits.
findings.finding: Success Criteria includes 'Prompt/routing CI drift-parity gate stays green after rubric edits' but no section of the plan describes any work, owner, or mechanism to achieve or maintain this gate — it is an uncovered success criterion with no corresponding described deliverable.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 4; plan body (all sections)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out/rollback path for rubric content changes (e.g., rubric versioning checkpoint, expand-contract merge sequence, or a defined revert procedure) so that a behavioral regression can be reversed without manual archaeology.
findings.finding: Rubric/pass content changes that alter reviewer behavior have no explicit rollback or back-out plan; 'advisory-by-default' limits blast radius but does not constitute a reversibility mechanism.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'No new blocking behavior ships without calibration evidence (advisory-by-default, consistent with existing gate posture)'
findings.suggested_fix: Before merging rubric edits, snapshot the prior rubric state (e.g., a versioned rubric file or a git-tag checkpoint). Define a one-step revert: 'restore prior rubric snapshot and re-run drift-parity CI.' Document this in the epic's Definition of Done so each child story inherits the pattern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a minimal calibration probe or eval fixture within this epic (or as an explicit dependency on spin-cub-usher before any child story closes) that specifies what 'calibration evidence' means, how it is measured, and what threshold unlocks blocking promotion — so SC-3 is verifiable at epic completion.
findings.finding: Success Criterion 3 requires 'calibration evidence' before any finding becomes blocking, but the mechanism for generating that evidence within this epic's scope is absent — R-4 (calibration-example library) is explicitly deferred to a separate placeholder epic (spin-cub-usher) with no concrete probe or measurement plan bridging the gap.
findings.location: Success Criteria SC-3 / Context section — 'R-4 (calibration-example library) -> placeholder epic spin-cub-usher'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one acceptance criterion to each child story that changes reviewer behavior: 'Run the rubric change against [N] representative plan samples; record TP/FP counts; attach to story before close.' This is the minimal empirical step that makes SC-3 verifiable without waiting for the full R-4 library.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the 'criterion-specific logic belongs in rubrics, not shared passes' boundary as an ADR or CLAUDE.md section, and reference it from this epic so future contributors know where to find the design rationale.
findings.finding: The plan articulates a novel architectural decision — 'criterion-specific logic lives in criterion rubrics/evidence, not in shared passes' — but names no ADR, CLAUDE.md section, or design-doc update to capture it durably. Without a recorded ADR, future maintainers may violate this boundary (e.g., adding criterion checks directly to Pass 2/4 prompts) without realizing it was an intentional constraint.
findings.location: Context / Guiding principle paragraph; Success Criteria item 2
findings.suggested_fix: Add an ADR entry (e.g., `docs/adr/0042-rubric-pass-separation.md`) that records: Decision, Context (DSO adoption), Consequences (shared passes stay generic; rubric YAML/markdown is the extension point), and Status. Link the ADR from both this epic and the child story backlog.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the Acceptance Criteria with the Success Criteria by adding a closing AC that requires all 10 child stories to reach 'implemented and eval-guarded' (or explicitly state that this epic closes on story-creation and a separate tracking mechanism owns implementation completion).
findings.finding: The Acceptance Criteria define success as '10 child stories CREATED' (observable at story-creation time), while the Success Criteria define success as '10 adopted workstreams IMPLEMENTED and eval-guarded' (observable only after implementation). An implementor could satisfy all ACs with empty/stub stories and claim the epic done, leaving the actual content upgrades undelivered.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria item 1 vs. Success Criteria item 1
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add AC: '10 child stories are implemented and their eval guards are green' to make this epic the true done-gate, or (b) explicitly note that this epic closes on story-creation and the implementation tracking lives in a separate dashboard/milestone, so the split is intentional and visible.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one concrete validation mechanism (e.g., before/after comparison of FP/FN rates on a held-out plan sample, a reviewer satisfaction signal, or a targeted eval showing behavioral change) to the Success Criteria so the epic's value is observable, not just internally gated.
findings.finding: No concrete validation mechanism for observable improvement to reviewer behavior is specified. The Success Criteria reference only internal system checks ('eval-guarded', 'CI drift-parity gate stays green'), but no before/after comparison of reviewer output quality, FP/FN rate targets, or dogfooding signal is present.
findings.location: Success Criteria section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a formal `depends_on` link from fort-wisp-wren to the R-1 part 1 child story (once created) so the sequencing constraint is machine-enforced, not prose-only; until that child story exists, note in fort-wisp-wren's ACs that it cannot be scheduled until the R-1 part 1 story is delivered.
findings.finding: The plan states R-1 part 1 (cohort observability field) is 'shipped here' — one of the 10 unenumerated workstreams — while fort-wisp-wren explicitly depends on it as a precondition ('Not to be implemented until (a) the cohort field ships'). However, fort-wisp-wren's `deps` record contains only a `relates_to` link to this epic, not a `depends_on` link to a specific R-1 part 1 child story. This is a prose-only ordering constraint with no formal enforcement: an implementer could pick up fort-wisp-wren before R-1 part 1 ships and encounter an unmet precondition with no gate to catch the sequencing violation.
findings.location: Context section — 'R-1 part 2 (blocking-tier context isolation) -> placeholder epic fort-wisp-wren (depends on R-1 part 1 cohort field, shipped here)'
findings.suggested_fix: When the R-1 part 1 child story is created as part of this epic's 10 workstreams, add a `depends_on` dep from fort-wisp-wren (ca57-1627-85ed-484c) to that child story's ticket_id. This converts the prose guard ('not to be implemented until the cohort field ships') into a schedulable dependency the `next_batch` / `validate` machinery can enforce.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief summary of the DSO bug-history evidence (or a stable link to it) directly in the epic description so the adopt/reject rationale is self-contained and satisfies the 'durable home' AC.
findings.finding: The plan's justification for preferring rebar's architecture over DSO's rests on 'DSO's own bug history validates it,' but the underlying evidence (the gap report at ~/dso_rebar_plan_review_gap_report.md) is an external, unlinkable document not reproduced or summarized in the plan. If the gap report's conclusions are later disputed, the adoption rationale has no durable grounding within rebar's own artifact store.
findings.location: Guiding principle section: 'DSO's own bug history validates it'
findings.suggested_fix: Under the Guiding Principle section, add a 2–4 sentence summary of the specific DSO failure modes (e.g., oscillation from self-scored committees, false positives from lexical word-match) that the bug history documented, and link to a stable rebar ticket or repo commit rather than a local file path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the 10 adopted DSO workstreams (at minimum: DSO gap ID, one-line scope, and which pass/rubric each affects) directly in the plan so an executor needs no external file to proceed.
findings.finding: The plan's primary deliverable — the '10 adopted workstreams' — is never enumerated or summarized in the plan itself; the only specification exists in an external file (~/dso_rebar_plan_review_gap_report.md) that is not version-controlled alongside the plan. An executor building or reviewing child stories cannot determine the 10 workstreams' scope, schema changes, or rubric modifications from the plan alone.
findings.location: Success Criteria / Acceptance Criteria — '10 child stories created'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a table to the plan body mapping each of the 10 workstreams to its DSO gap-report ID, the rebar pass it touches (Pass-1 rubric, Pass-2 contract, Pass-4 MOVE_REGISTRY, sidecar schema, etc.), and whether it is content-only or requires a schema change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the R-1 part 1 cohort field: specify its name (e.g. `pass1_chunk`), type (sorted criterion-id list), placement in the finding dict output from `pass1_chunk`, and the corresponding sidecar `_slim()` key so fort-wisp-wren's evidence-gate precondition is verifiable.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'R-1 part 1 cohort field, shipped here' as a prerequisite that fort-wisp-wren (R-1 part 2) depends on, but tool-verification confirms the cohort field does not yet exist: pass1_chunk's output dict (passes.py:294-306) and sidecar._slim (sidecar.py:236-264) contain no cohort or pass1_chunk field. The field's name, placement in the finding dict, and sidecar schema slot are all unspecified in the plan.
findings.location: Context section — 'R-1 part 2 (blocking-tier context isolation) → placeholder epic fort-wisp-wren (depends on R-1 part 1 cohort field, shipped here)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan (or a child story AC): `pass1_chunk` stamps each finding with a `_cohort` field (sorted list of criterion ids in the chunk, e.g. `["E1","T8"]`) in its output dict; `sidecar._slim()` persists it as `cohort`; fort-wisp-wren can then group sidecar findings by cohort to measure co-residency contamination.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'eval-guarded' for this epic: specify which existing eval spec(s) must pass (or what new spec is required), the threshold, and the condition under which a workstream is exempt (e.g. metadata-only changes that do not alter prompt content).
findings.finding: The Success Criterion 'eval-guarded where it changes reviewer behavior' is referenced as a gate for all 10 workstreams but the plan never defines what constitutes 'eval-guarded': which eval spec, what pass/fail threshold, what schema the eval result must satisfy, or how behavioral change is measured. The criterion is a decision rule applied to every workstream without a co-located definition.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Each of the 10 adopted workstreams (child stories) is implemented and eval-guarded where it changes reviewer behavior'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign cross-epic linking (grumpy-gland-rose, only-crave-art, fort-wisp-wren, spin-cub-usher) to a child story or add an explicit AC to an existing child (WS9 or WS10 are natural candidates).
findings.finding: Parent acceptance criterion 'Related design/placeholder epics linked (grumpy-gland-rose, only-crave-art, fort-wisp-wren, spin-cub-usher)' is uncovered by any child. No child has an AC or deliverable for creating or verifying these cross-epic links. WS9 references fort-wisp-wren directionally ('Unblocks the evidence gate for fort-wisp-wren') but does not own the linking task.
findings.location: child 8656-92dc-f6eb-4a8f (closest reference); parent acceptance criterion 2
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC to WS10 (docs + authoring surface, already the housekeeping child): '[ ] All four related epics (grumpy-gland-rose, only-crave-art, fort-wisp-wren, spin-cub-usher) linked from this epic with a one-line rationale per link.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an eval-guard AC to each of WS1, WS2, WS3, WS4, and WS8 confirming that the changed reviewer behavior does not regress existing passing/failing eval cases.
findings.finding: Five children that materially change reviewer behavior (WS1, WS2, WS3, WS4, WS8) lack an explicit eval-guard acceptance criterion, partially contradicting the parent success criterion 'eval-guarded where it changes reviewer behavior'.
findings.location: child 80b3-6a4e-8a20-4090 (WS1), child 4ff8-8d1d-ac17-4ed2 (WS2), child 1733-6b71-e83d-49c3 (WS3), child b955-82d0-e476-46da (WS4), child 315d-b4ba-6c9e-482d (WS8)
findings.suggested_fix: For each of the five children, add an AC of the form: '[ ] Eval/sidecar regression confirms [new mechanism] does not suppress genuine findings or introduce new FP classes vs. the pre-ship baseline.' WS1 can reuse its existing calibration AC as the basis; WS2–WS4 and WS8 need net-new eval items.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify ownership of the G4 consumer-enumeration worklist idiom: designate one child (WS3 or WS5) as the implementor and the other as the consumer, with an explicit cross-reference AC.
findings.finding: G4 consumer-enumeration precursor is claimed as a deliverable by both WS3 and WS5 without clear ownership delineation, creating a scope overlap where neither child may fully implement it or both may implement divergent versions.
findings.suggested_fix: WS3 (as the overlay mechanism child) should own the worklist idiom implementation since it introduces the recall->worklist pattern for enumeration. WS5 should reference WS3's mechanism explicitly: 'G4 consumer-enumeration AC in WS5 is satisfied by WS3's worklist idiom; WS5 adds only the rubric note directing reviewers to use it.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit ordering/dependency AC to WS2: 'Requires WS1 (committed_work_relies_on_unbacked_claim) to be shipped and live in the verifier contract before WS2 hedge-detector routing is active.'
findings.finding: WS2 routes its hedge-detector findings to WS1's committed_work_relies_on_unbacked_claim sub-answer but has no ordering constraint ensuring WS1 ships first, creating a dependency gap where WS2 could ship against a verifier contract that does not yet include the target routing key.
findings.location: child 4ff8-8d1d-ac17-4ed2 (WS2)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to WS2 ACs: '[ ] Blocked on WS1: hedge-detector routing to committed_work_relies_on_unbacked_claim is only activated after WS1's verifier contract expansion has shipped and been validated.' Alternatively, include WS1+WS2 in a single combined deployment gate.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
